check.meta_title: Asbestos Risk Assessment Tool — Free Checker by Country & Era
check.meta_description: Free asbestos risk checker: does your home have asbestos? Enter country and year built for instant results. Asbestos risk by decade. No registration.
check.title: Asbestos Risk Checker
check.materials_none: No specific materials identified for this profile
check.disclaimer_note: This assessment is for awareness only. It is NOT a substitute for professional asbestos inspection.
check.awareness_callout: This is a general awareness tool based on construction patterns in your country and era. It is NOT an assessment of your specific building. Only a certified professional inspection can determine if asbestos is present.
check.find_inspector_title: 🔍 Learn about asbestos in your country
check.find_inspector_desc: View regulations, common materials, and recommended next steps for your country.
actions_low: No immediate action needed if materials are undisturbed
Keep records of any known or suspected asbestos materials
actions_moderate: Schedule a professional asbestos survey before any renovation
Do not sand, drill, or disturb suspected materials
Contact a licensed professional if you find damaged materials
actions_high: Contact a licensed asbestos assessor for a full inspection
Do not perform any drilling, sanding, or renovation
Seal off any visibly damaged areas temporarily
actions_critical: Contact a certified asbestos abatement contractor immediately
Do not disturb any building materials
Consult local health authorities for guidance

country.title: Country Profile
country.what_to_do: What To Do If You Live Here
country.common_materials_intro: Materials commonly found in buildings from this era:
country.more_info_coming: Detailed information for this country is being compiled. Check back soon.
country.cta_description: Enter your building's age and type for a personalized asbestos risk assessment.
country.page_disclaimer: Information aggregated from public sources including IBAS, EPA, and WHO. Not legal or medical advice.
what_to_do_full_ban: Your country has banned asbestos, but older buildings may still contain legacy materials.
Buildings built before the ban year may contain asbestos-containing materials.
Hire a certified asbestos surveyor before any renovation or demolition work.
Do not disturb older building materials without professional testing.
what_to_do_no_ban: Asbestos is still legally used in your country.
New and older construction alike may contain asbestos-containing materials.
Consult a certified professional before any renovation work.
Advocate for stronger asbestos regulations in your region.
what_to_do_partial_ban: Some uses of asbestos are still permitted in your country.
Older and some newer buildings may contain asbestos materials.
Always consult a certified professional before renovating.
Check local regulations for specific exemptions in your area.
what_to_do_unknown: Regulatory status in your country is unclear.
Exercise caution with all older buildings.
Consult a certified professional before any renovation work.
Verify local regulations with your national health authority.
country.not_found_description: This country profile is not in our database.
country.resistance_subtitle: The people who fought for change.
country.material_guide_subtitle: Common materials still present in buildings
country.key_figures_subtitle: Context and sources behind the numbers
country.key_figures_ban_full: All forms of asbestos have been banned since {year}. Buildings constructed before this date may still contain asbestos materials.
country.key_figures_ban_partial: Asbestos is partially regulated. Some forms remain legal, meaning exposure risk persists in both old and newer construction.
country.key_figures_ban_none: No national ban exists. Asbestos may still be used in new construction and is present in buildings of all ages.
country.key_figures_ban_unknown: Regulatory status is unclear. Exercise caution with all buildings, especially those built before 2000.
country.key_figures_meso_description: {rate} cases per million people per year. Mesothelioma is an aggressive cancer caused almost exclusively by asbestos exposure, with a latency period of 20–50 years.
country.key_figures_meso_high: This is among the highest rates globally, reflecting decades of heavy industrial asbestos use.
country.key_figures_meso_moderate: This rate indicates significant historical asbestos exposure in the population.
country.key_figures_meso_low: A lower rate, though any mesothelioma cases indicate past asbestos exposure.
country.key_figures_buildings_description: Estimated scope of asbestos-containing materials still present in the built environment.
country.key_figures_peak_description: The period when asbestos was most heavily used in construction. Buildings from this era have the highest probability of containing asbestos materials.

what_is_page.title: What is Asbestos?
what_is_page.meta_description: What is asbestos? Learn about the 3 fiber types (chrysotile, amosite, crocidolite), why asbestos is dangerous, and the diseases it causes including mesothelioma.
what_is_page.intro: Asbestos is a group of naturally occurring silicate minerals made of long, thin fibrous crystals. For much of the 20th century, it was valued for its heat resistance, tensile strength, and low cost. Its fibers are invisible to the naked eye — and that is what makes them deadly.

fiber_types.name: Chrysotile (White Asbestos)
fiber_types.description: Accounts for about 95% of all asbestos ever used commercially. Curly, flexible fibers. Found in roofing, brake pads, insulation, and floor tiles. Still legally used in about 60 countries.

fiber_types.name: Amosite (Brown Asbestos)
fiber_types.description: Straight, needle-like fibers. Primarily used in thermal insulation boards and ceiling tiles. More brittle than chrysotile — breaks into smaller, sharper particles. Associated with higher mesothelioma risk.

fiber_types.name: Crocidolite (Blue Asbestos)
fiber_types.description: The most hazardous type. Very thin, straight fibers penetrate deep into lung tissue. Used in spray-on insulation and some cement products. Most countries banned its use first.

what_is_page.why_dangerous_intro: Two properties make asbestos uniquely dangerous among industrial minerals:
what_is_page.friability_body: When disturbed, asbestos-containing materials crumble and release microscopic fibers into the air. These fibers are 700 times thinner than a human hair. They are invisible, odorless, and tasteless — you cannot detect an exposure as it happens.
what_is_page.biopersistence_body: Once inhaled, asbestos fibers lodge in lung tissue and stay there permanently. The human body cannot break them down. A single exposure event can trigger disease 20 to 50 years later — which is why asbestos deaths are still rising in countries that banned it decades ago.
what_is_page.mechanism_body: When the immune system detects a fiber it cannot destroy, it repeatedly attacks it in a process called frustrated phagocytosis. This chronic inflammation scars the lung tissue and, over years, can cause genetic damage in surrounding cells — the starting point for mesothelioma and other cancers.

diseases.name: Mesothelioma
diseases.description: A rare, aggressive cancer of the membrane lining the lungs, abdomen, or heart. Virtually always caused by asbestos. Median survival after diagnosis: 12–21 months. No cure.

diseases.name: Asbestosis
diseases.description: Chronic scarring of lung tissue caused by long-term asbestos exposure. Causes progressive shortness of breath. Irreversible. Can lead to respiratory failure.

diseases.name: Lung Cancer
diseases.description: Asbestos is a proven cause of lung cancer, independent of smoking. Risk multiplies dramatically when combined with tobacco. Heavy smokers with asbestos exposure face a 50× higher risk than the general population.

what_is_page.no_ban_countries_body: These major countries have no national asbestos ban. Billions of people live in buildings that may contain asbestos-containing materials.
what_is_page.disclaimer: Information aggregated from WHO, EPA, and IARC public sources. Not medical advice.
what_is_page.cta_body: Find out if your building may contain asbestos based on its country, age, and type.

where_hides_page.title: Where Does Asbestos Hide in Your Home?
where_hides_page.meta_description: Where is asbestos found in homes? Complete guide to asbestos building materials organized by location — floors, walls, ceilings, and pipes.
where_hides_page.intro: Asbestos was used in over 3,000 different products. It does not have a distinctive look. The only way to confirm its presence is professional laboratory testing. This guide helps you know which materials deserve extra caution.
where_hides_page.friable_warning: Friable materials release fibers more easily when touched or damaged.
where_hides_page.cta_body: Use the Risk Checker to get a personalized assessment based on your country and building age.
where_hides_page.disclaimer: Material data sourced from EPA and WHO. Presence in any specific building can only be confirmed by professional sampling and laboratory analysis.

history_page.title: How Industry Hid the Truth
history_page.meta_description: The asbestos cover-up: how the industry suppressed evidence of harm for decades. Timeline from 1906 to the 2024 U.S. ban. A documented history.
history_page.intro: This is not a story about ignorance. By the 1930s, the asbestos industry had internal studies proving their product killed workers. What followed was one of the most consequential cover-ups in industrial history.

events.event: French factory inspector Auribault reports 50 deaths in an asbestos weaving factory. It is one of the first documented accounts of asbestos-related occupational death.

Johns-Manville, the world's largest asbestos company, commissions the Lanza Report — internal research confirming asbestosis in workers. The company suppresses publication and alters findings before any limited release.
Germany becomes the first country in the world to recognize mesothelioma as an occupational disease caused by asbestos and compensates affected workers.
British epidemiologist Richard Doll publishes the landmark study linking asbestos exposure directly to lung cancer, with a 10× elevated risk for textile workers.
Dr. Irving Selikoff presents landmark research at a New York Academy of Sciences conference: insulation workers face catastrophically elevated cancer risk. The study cannot be ignored.
Internal Bendix Corporation memo: 'We doubt that we will be able to be competitive if we have to remove asbestos from our products.' The company continues using asbestos.
The U.S. EPA issues the Asbestos Ban and Phase-Out Rule — intended to ban most asbestos products. The most sweeping domestic asbestos regulation in American history.
The Fifth Circuit Court of Appeals overturns most of the 1989 EPA ban, ruling the agency did not adequately weigh economic costs. Asbestos remains legal in the U.S. for most uses.
The World Health Organization calls for a global ban on all forms of asbestos, citing an estimated 90,000 deaths per year from occupational exposure alone.
The U.S. EPA finalizes the Chrysotile Asbestos Rule under TSCA, banning the last commercial use of asbestos in the United States — 33 years after the 1991 court setback.

history_page.closing_body: Even in countries with complete bans, asbestos installed before the ban year remains in place in millions of buildings. Legacy exposure is now the dominant cause of new mesothelioma diagnoses in high-income countries. In countries without bans, the first wave of occupational deaths is still to come.
history_page.disclaimer: All events sourced from peer-reviewed literature, government publications, and court records.

what_to_do_page.title: What To Do If You Find Asbestos
what_to_do_page.meta_description: Found asbestos in your home? What to do and what NOT to do. Step-by-step guide for suspecting, assessing, and confirmed asbestos — when to call a professional.
what_to_do_page.intro: The most important rule: do not panic, and do not touch. Intact asbestos-containing materials that are left undisturbed pose minimal immediate risk. The danger comes from disturbance.

scenario_suspect.title: Scenario 1: I Suspect Asbestos
steps: Stop any work in the area immediately. Do not drill, sand, cut, or disturb the material.
Identify the material and note its location, condition (intact or damaged), and estimated age of the building.
Do not take samples yourself. Collecting samples without professional equipment can release fibers.
If the material is damaged and you see visible dust or crumbling, leave the room and close the door.
Contact a certified asbestos surveyor for a professional assessment. Ask for an accredited lab analysis.

scenario_professional.title: Scenario 2: A Professional Is Coming
steps: Make sure the surveyor is licensed. Ask for their accreditation number before they start.
Keep the area clear. Remove people and pets from the room being assessed.
Ask for a written report with specific material locations, condition ratings, and lab results.
Request a risk classification: is the material 'manage in place', 'encapsulate', or 'remove'?
Keep all reports permanently. Disclose findings to future buyers or tenants.

scenario_confirmed.title: Scenario 3: Asbestos Has Been Confirmed
steps: Read the surveyor's recommendation carefully: not all confirmed asbestos requires removal.
If the recommendation is 'manage in place', schedule annual inspections and keep records.
If removal is required, hire a licensed asbestos abatement contractor — not a general contractor.
Do not re-enter the work area during removal. Ensure the contractor uses proper containment and air monitoring.
Request a clearance certificate after removal, including air quality test results.
Dispose of waste through licensed hazardous waste channels. Never put asbestos debris in regular trash.

do_not_items: Do NOT sand, grind, drill, or cut suspected materials
Do NOT use a household vacuum or broom to clean up asbestos debris
Do NOT remove asbestos-containing materials yourself — even with a dust mask
Do NOT put asbestos waste in regular household garbage
Do NOT ignore damaged or crumbling materials in older buildings
Do NOT allow children to play near visibly damaged ceiling tiles, walls, or floor tiles in old buildings
what_to_do_page.when_to_call_heading: When To Call a Professional Immediately
when_to_call_items: You see crumbling, damaged, or water-stained materials in a pre-1990 building
You are planning any renovation, drilling, or structural work in a building built before 1990
A child or family member has been exposed to visible dust from building materials
You are buying, selling, or renting a building constructed before 1990
what_to_do_page.disclaimer: This guide provides general awareness information only. Always consult a certified asbestos professional for your specific situation. ToxinFree is not responsible for actions taken based on this guide.

numbers_page.title: Asbestos by the Numbers
numbers_page.meta_description: Asbestos deaths per year, global ban statistics, and regional exposure data. 255,000 deaths annually. 72 countries with bans. Data from WHO, IBAS, EPA.
numbers_page.intro: The numbers behind the global asbestos crisis. All statistics sourced from WHO, IBAS, and peer-reviewed epidemiological research.
numbers_page.stat_deaths_label: people die each year from asbestos-related diseases
numbers_page.stat_banned_label: countries have a full or effective ban on asbestos
numbers_page.stat_no_ban_label: countries still have no comprehensive asbestos ban
numbers_page.stat_roofing_label: km² of asbestos-cement roofing still in use worldwide
numbers_page.chart_region_description: Number of countries with and without a comprehensive asbestos ban, grouped by region.
numbers_page.chart_distribution_description: Share of countries by regulatory status, as of 2024.
numbers_page.disclaimer: Statistics compiled from WHO, IBAS, and U.S. EPA public databases. Country-level regulatory data may have a lag of 12–24 months.

methodology_page.title: How We Source Our Data
methodology_page.meta_description: Learn how ToxinFree sources asbestos ban data for 200 countries — sources, methodology, curation process, and how to report errors.
methodology_page.intro: ToxinFree aggregates publicly available data from authoritative international sources. We do not conduct original research. Here is exactly how our data is collected, curated, and updated.
methodology_page.source_ibas_desc: The global reference for asbestos ban legislation. Maintained by Laurie Kazan-Allen, IBAS tracks national bans, partial bans, and regulatory milestones for 195+ countries.
methodology_page.source_who_desc: Mesothelioma mortality rates and global health statistics on asbestos-related diseases.
methodology_page.source_epa_desc: U.S. asbestos regulations, banned product lists, and building safety guidelines.
methodology_page.source_usgs_desc: Global asbestos production and consumption data by country and mineral type.
methodology_page.source_un_comtrade_desc: International trade data used to cross-reference asbestos import/export volumes.
process_steps: Ban status and timeline data is compiled from IBAS published lists (last referenced February 2026).
Country-level data is cross-referenced against WHO, EPA, and UN Comtrade where available.
Data is reviewed for consistency and flagged entries are marked as 'unknown' when sources conflict.
All data points include a source URL linking to the original official document.
methodology_page.data_disclaimer: Our initial data set was assembled with AI assistance to process and structure publicly available regulatory information. While we have reviewed this data for accuracy, it may contain errors or omissions. We are a small team and cannot guarantee real-time accuracy. Always verify critical regulatory information against official government sources before making decisions.
methodology_page.error_report: If you find an error in our data, please contact us at data@toxinfree.global — include the country name, the incorrect data, and the correct source URL.
